subsections.title: JPA Fundamentals & Entity Lifecycle
subsections.keyRule: JPA = Java Persistence API (spec). Hibernate = most popular JPA implementation. Entity lifecycle: Transient (not managed) → Persistent (managed by EntityManager) → Detached (was managed) → Removed. Changes to Persistent entities auto-saved on transaction commit — 'dirty checking'.

terms.what: @Entity marks a Java class as a JPA entity — a class that maps to a database table. @Table specifies the exact table name.
terms.why: Without @Entity, JPA does not manage the class. @Entity tells Hibernate: 'manage this class, map it to a table, auto-persist changes'.
terms.how: @Entity @Table(name='users') public class User { @Id @GeneratedValue Long id; @Column(name='full_name') String fullName; }
terms.where: Every domain class that has a corresponding DB table in your app.
terms.when: Use @Entity for persistent domain objects. Use @Embeddable for value objects (address, money) embedded in an entity.
terms.issue: Missing @Entity on a class used in a JPA repository = 'Not a managed type' exception at startup.
terms.fix: Add @Entity and @Id to every persistent class. Ensure it has a no-arg constructor (JPA requires it for proxy creation).

star.s: JPA repository not working — 'Not a managed type'.

terms.what: @Id marks the primary key field. @GeneratedValue specifies how the ID is generated: IDENTITY (auto-increment), SEQUENCE (DB sequence), UUID, TABLE.
terms.why: Every JPA entity must have a primary key. Generation strategy determines how unique IDs are created.
terms.how: @Id @GeneratedValue(strategy=GenerationType.IDENTITY) Long id — MySQL auto-increment. SEQUENCE — better for PostgreSQL with batch inserts. UUID — good for distributed systems.
terms.when: Use IDENTITY for MySQL. Use SEQUENCE for PostgreSQL (more efficient batch inserts). Use UUID for distributed systems where you need ID before DB insert.
terms.issue: IDENTITY strategy requires a DB roundtrip per insert — cannot batch inserts efficiently. For bulk inserts of 10,000 rows, this is slow.
terms.fix: Use SEQUENCE with allocationSize=50 — Hibernate pre-allocates 50 IDs at a time and hands them out without DB calls. Batch inserts become fast.

star.s: Bulk import of 50,000 records takes 5 minutes.
star.a: IDENTITY strategy caused 50,000 DB roundtrips for IDs. Changed to SEQUENCE with allocationSize=50. Enabled JDBC batch.

terms.what: Lazy loading = related entities loaded only when accessed (on demand). Eager loading = related entities loaded immediately with the parent query.
terms.why: Lazy loading avoids loading unused data — better performance. But loading a lazy association outside a transaction causes LazyInitializationException.
terms.how: @OneToMany(fetch=FetchType.LAZY) — default for collections. @ManyToOne(fetch=FetchType.EAGER) — default for single entities. Override per-query with JOIN FETCH.
terms.when: Use LAZY everywhere by default. Eagerly load only when you always need the association. Never change global default to EAGER — causes N+1 and performance issues.

terms.what: N+1 is a performance trap: one query gets N parent records, then N more queries fetch each child collection — total N+1 queries instead of 1.
terms.why: Lazy loading looks safe but when you iterate N parents and access their collections, each access triggers a separate query.
terms.how: 50 orders loaded → loop accesses order.getItems() → 50 separate queries for items = 51 queries total. Should be 2 (one for orders, one for all items with IN clause).
terms.where: Any @OneToMany or @ManyToMany with lazy loading accessed inside loops. Very common in REST endpoints that serialize entity graphs.
terms.when: Always watch for N+1 in any loop that accesses lazy associations. Enable SQL logging in dev to detect: spring.jpa.show-sql=true.
terms.issue: N+1 can make a page load that should take 5ms take 500ms or more under real data volumes.
terms.fix: 1) JOIN FETCH: 'SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items' — loads both in one query. 2) @EntityGraph on the repository method. 3) Batch loading with @BatchSize(size=20). 4) Use DTO projections.

star.s: Product listing page slows under load.
star.a: Spring show-sql shows 1 + 200 queries for 200 products and their categories. Added JOIN FETCH.

terms.what: @Transactional on a Spring method starts a transaction at the beginning and commits (or rolls back) at the end. Dirty checking saves any changes to persistent entities automatically.
terms.why: Without @Transactional, Hibernate cannot track entity changes or manage session lifecycle. Also groups multiple operations into one atomic unit.
terms.how: @Transactional public void updateUser(Long id, String name) { User user = repo.findById(id).get(); user.setName(name); // no save() call needed — dirty checking auto-saves on commit }
terms.where: Every service method that writes to DB. @Transactional(readOnly=true) for queries — enables read optimizations.
terms.when: Service layer should own transaction boundaries. Do NOT put @Transactional on controller or repository methods. readOnly=true for all queries.
terms.issue: @Transactional self-invocation: calling a @Transactional method from the SAME class bypasses the proxy — no transaction starts.
terms.fix: Call @Transactional methods from a different bean. Or inject self-reference (use carefully). Or restructure to avoid self-call.

star.s: User balance update and audit log must be atomic.
star.a: @Transactional service method does both. If audit log fails, balance update rolls back.

terms.what: JPQL (JPA Query Language) is SQL-like but works on entity classes and fields, not table/column names. Criteria API is a type-safe, programmatic query builder.
terms.why: JPQL is database-independent — the same query works on MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle. Criteria API prevents typos in field names at compile time.
terms.how: @Query('SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email') — JPQL. CriteriaBuilder cb = em.getCriteriaBuilder(); CriteriaQuery<User> cq = cb.createQuery(User.class); — Criteria API.
terms.where: Spring Data JPA repositories. Any complex query with joins, aggregations, or conditional clauses.

terms.what: L1 Cache = Hibernate Session cache — per-request. Objects fetched once are cached for the session duration. L2 Cache = shared across sessions — needs explicit configuration (EhCache, Redis).
terms.why: L1 cache prevents duplicate DB queries within one request. L2 cache avoids repeated DB queries for the same data across requests.
terms.how: L1 is automatic — within one @Transactional method, findById(1) twice = one DB query. L2 needs: @Cache(usage=READ_WRITE) on entity + cache provider.
terms.where: L1 = always active. L2 = reference data (country list, config, product catalog) that rarely changes.
terms.when: L1 is always on. Enable L2 for: rarely-changing reference data, data fetched many times per second, read-heavy entities.
terms.issue: L2 cache serves stale data if DB updated directly (not through JPA). Bypassed with native SQL also bypasses cache.
terms.fix: Always update data through JPA — cache gets evicted. For L2 with direct DB updates, add explicit cache eviction calls or short TTL.

star.s: Country list (200 records) fetched on every API request — DB query every time.
star.a: Added @Cache(READ_ONLY) on Country entity + EhCache config.
star.r: First request hits DB, all subsequent from cache. DB load for country reduced to near zero.

subsections.title: JPA Extended
subsections.keyRule: ORM maps Java objects to DB tables. EntityManager manages entity lifecycle. @MappedSuperclass for shared base class fields. @Embeddable for value objects embedded in entity tables. @Inheritance for class hierarchies. Cascade types control which operations propagate from parent to child. @EntityGraph solves N+1 for specific queries.

terms.what: ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) maps Java objects to database tables automatically. JPA is the Java ORM standard. Hibernate is the most popular JPA implementation.
terms.why: Without ORM, developers write boilerplate SQL and ResultSet mapping for every field. ORM automates this, letting you work with objects.
terms.how: @Entity class User maps to 'user' table. EntityManager.persist(user) = INSERT. EntityManager.find(User.class, id) = SELECT. Changes to attached entity = UPDATE automatically.
terms.where: Data persistence layer in Spring Boot apps. Spring Data JPA builds on JPA with repositories.
terms.when: Use JPA for standard CRUD. For complex queries, use JPQL, Criteria API, or native SQL. For read-heavy reporting, consider plain JDBC or QueryDSL.
terms.issue: JPA magic (auto dirty checking, lazy loading, cascades) can cause unexpected SQL or performance issues if you don't understand what's happening.
terms.fix: Enable SQL logging (spring.jpa.show-sql=true) during development. Understand what JPA does before letting it do it.

terms.what: EntityManager is the JPA API for all persistence operations. persist(), find(), merge(), remove(), detach(), flush(), refresh(). Each operation changes entity lifecycle state.
terms.why: Understanding EntityManager methods prevents data corruption and unexpected behavior.
terms.how: em.persist(entity) — NEW → MANAGED (will be INSERTed). em.find(cls,id) — loads entity → MANAGED. em.merge(detached) — DETACHED → MANAGED (copy). em.remove(entity) — MANAGED → REMOVED (will be DELETEd). em.flush() — writes pending changes to DB now.
terms.where: Spring Data JPA hides most EntityManager usage. You use it directly in custom repositories or complex operations.
terms.when: Know merge() vs persist(): persist() fails for detached entities. merge() is for updating detached entities returned from API calls.
terms.issue: Calling persist() on a detached entity (one that came from a different EntityManager or transaction) throws 'detached entity passed to persist'.
terms.fix: Use merge() for entities that may have been detached. Use persist() only for brand new entities.

star.a: Entity was loaded in one transaction, passed to another. Changed persist() to merge().

terms.what: @Column customizes the mapping: name, nullable, length, unique, updatable. @Transient marks a field to NOT be persisted — excluded from DB mapping.
terms.why: Default column names may differ from Java convention. @Transient lets you have computed or temporary fields in entity without DB columns.
terms.how: @Column(name='user_email', nullable=false, unique=true, length=255) String email; @Transient String displayName; // not in DB — computed in getter.
terms.where: Entity classes that need custom column names (DB uses snake_case, Java uses camelCase).
terms.when: Use @Column when: DB column name differs from field name, need length/nullable constraints, or need unique constraint.
terms.issue: @Transient field initialized in constructor — after loading from DB, the field is reset to null/default since JPA doesn't populate it.
terms.fix: Compute @Transient fields lazily in getter: if(displayName==null) displayName = firstName + ' ' + lastName;

star.s: Entity has computed 'fullName' field but JPA tries to save it to DB — column not found.
star.r: Field excluded from DB operations. Computed on demand.

terms.what: @Embeddable marks a class as an embeddable value object. @Embedded includes it in the owning entity's table. No separate table — fields are columns in the parent table.
terms.why: Value objects (Address, Money, DateRange) share no identity — they belong to an entity. Embed them to avoid unnecessary join tables.
terms.how: @Embeddable class Address { String street; String city; } @Entity class User { @Embedded Address address; } — address fields become columns in USER table.
terms.where: Addresses, monetary amounts, date ranges, phone numbers, coordinates — any value object.
terms.when: Use @Embeddable when value object does not need its own identity and will always be part of an entity.
terms.issue: Two @Embedded fields of same @Embeddable type in one entity causes column name conflict.

terms.what: @MappedSuperclass marks a class whose mappings are applied to its subclasses, but it has no table of its own. Common fields (id, createdAt, updatedAt) go in the superclass.
terms.why: Avoids repeating common audit fields (createdAt, updatedAt, createdBy) in every entity class.
terms.how: @MappedSuperclass abstract class BaseEntity { @Id @GeneratedValue Long id; LocalDateTime createdAt; } @Entity class User extends BaseEntity { ... } — User gets id and createdAt.
terms.where: Auditing base class shared by all entities, common fields like version, createdAt, updatedAt.
terms.when: Use when multiple entities share common fields. Combine with JPA Auditing (@EnableJpaAuditing, @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate) for automatic population.
terms.issue: @MappedSuperclass cannot be queried directly — only through subclass entities.
terms.fix: For polymorphic queries across entity types, use @Inheritance instead of @MappedSuperclass.

star.s: 15 entity classes each repeat id, createdAt, updatedAt.
star.a: @MappedSuperclass BaseEntity with those fields. All entities extend it.
star.r: Fields removed from 15 classes. One place to change.

terms.what: @Inheritance defines how a class hierarchy maps to DB. Three strategies: SINGLE_TABLE (all subclasses in one table), TABLE_PER_CLASS (each subclass has own table), JOINED (parent table + subclass table joined).
terms.why: Need to persist a class hierarchy — Vehicle, Car, Truck. Database doesn't have inheritance — need a strategy.
terms.where: Any entity class hierarchy. Most common: SINGLE_TABLE for performance. JOINED for normalized schema.
terms.when: SINGLE_TABLE: best performance (no joins), wastes space with nulls. JOINED: normalized, slower queries. TABLE_PER_CLASS: fastest for single type, cannot query polymorphically without UNION.
terms.issue: SINGLE_TABLE with many subclasses creates a wide table with many nullable columns — schema gets messy.
terms.fix: JOINED strategy for normalized design. SINGLE_TABLE for performance-critical small hierarchies.

star.a: SINGLE_TABLE if vehicles always queried together and types are few. JOINED if schema must be clean and types differ significantly.

terms.what: @OneToOne: each entity relates to exactly one of the other. @ManyToMany: each entity relates to multiple of the other (requires join table).
terms.why: Model complex relationships. @OneToOne for profile→user. @ManyToMany for student↔course.
terms.how: @OneToOne: @JoinColumn(name='profile_id') Profile profile; @ManyToMany: @JoinTable(name='student_course', joinColumns=..., inverseJoinColumns=...) Set<Course> courses;

terms.what: Cascade types control which operations propagate from parent to child entity: PERSIST, MERGE, REMOVE, REFRESH, DETACH, ALL. Without cascade, you must manually manage each related entity.
terms.why: When you save an Order, you usually want its OrderItems saved too — CascadeType.PERSIST does this automatically.
terms.how: @OneToMany(cascade = {CascadeType.PERSIST, CascadeType.MERGE}) List<OrderItem> items; — persist and merge cascade. CascadeType.ALL = all operations cascade.
terms.where: Parent-child entity relationships: Order→OrderItem, Blog→Comment, User→Address.
terms.when: Use cascade for true parent-child relationships where child cannot exist without parent. Avoid cascade REMOVE on shared entities.
terms.issue: CascadeType.REMOVE on a shared entity (like Category referenced by many Products) deletes the Category when one Product is deleted.
terms.fix: Only use REMOVE cascade when child truly cannot exist without the specific parent. Use orphanRemoval for one-parent relationships.

star.s: Deleting one product accidentally deletes the shared category.
star.a: Removed CascadeType.REMOVE. Products are not the parent of Category.

terms.what: @BatchSize(size=20) tells Hibernate to load lazy collections in batches of 20 instead of one per entity — reduces N+1 queries. @EntityGraph specifies which associations to fetch eagerly for a specific query.
terms.why: N+1 problem: loading 100 orders then accessing each order's items = 101 queries. @BatchSize reduces to ceil(100/20)+1 = 6 queries. @EntityGraph = control eager loading per query.
terms.how: @BatchSize(size=20) List<Item> items; — lazy but batched. @EntityGraph(attributePaths={'items','customer'}) in repository method — fetches items and customer in JOIN.
terms.where: Any lazy-loaded collection in entity. @EntityGraph on Spring Data JPA repository methods.
terms.when: @BatchSize: easy fix for N+1 when specific query tuning not needed. @EntityGraph: targeted fix for specific use cases that need specific associations.
terms.issue: @EntityGraph eagerly fetching large collections causes CartesianProduct — huge result sets.
terms.fix: Use @EntityGraph with subgraph for targeted fetching. For large collections, use separate JPQL query with JOIN FETCH instead.

star.s: Report page loads 100 orders — takes 30 seconds.

terms.what: JPA lifecycle callbacks: @PrePersist, @PostPersist, @PreUpdate, @PostUpdate, @PreRemove, @PostRemove, @PostLoad. Methods annotated with these are called at the corresponding lifecycle events.
terms.why: Auto-set timestamps, auto-populate audit fields, validate before save, trigger side effects — without explicit service code.
terms.how: @PrePersist void onCreate() { this.createdAt = LocalDateTime.now(); } @PreUpdate void onUpdate() { this.updatedAt = LocalDateTime.now(); }

terms.what: Named Query: @NamedQuery pre-compiles JPQL at startup — validated early. @Modifying: marks a query as update/delete — required for non-SELECT JPQL. Pagination: Pageable parameter with Page<T> return.
terms.why: Named queries fail fast at startup if JPQL is wrong. @Modifying allows bulk updates. Pagination prevents loading millions of rows into memory.
terms.how: @Modifying @Query('UPDATE User u SET u.active = false WHERE u.lastLogin < :date') void deactivateOldUsers(@Param('date') LocalDate date); Pageable p = PageRequest.of(0, 20); Page<User> page = repo.findAll(p);
terms.where: Spring Data JPA repositories. Bulk operations. Paged REST endpoints.
terms.when: Use @Modifying for bulk updates. Always paginate API endpoints that return lists. Use @NamedQuery for commonly-used complex queries.
terms.fix: Always combine @Modifying with @Transactional. Also clear the persistence context: @Modifying(clearAutomatically=true).

star.s: Bulk deactivation of 1M old accounts via loop takes 2 hours.
